text: The surgeon begins by dividing the gastrocolic ligament to access the lesser sac, moving towards the duodenum. The fused gastrocolic ligament is divided to expose the right gastroepiploic vessels. The duodenum and uncinate process are mobilized, revealing a branch of the vein to the colon, which is divided. The right gastroepiploic artery is identified and cauterized due to its small size and sufficient length. The remaining ligament attached to the first portion of the duodenum is divided, and smaller gastroepiploic veins are cauterized.

The surgeon continues to expose the first portion of the duodenum, dissecting towards the lesser curvature. The gastroduodenal artery (GDA) is clearly visible, with small bleeders being controlled. Dissection proceeds towards the gastro-hepatic ligament, and the duodenum is further dissected until reaching the opposite side, where the liver is visible.

The surgeon clears the duodenum below the pylorus and uses a GIA stapler to divide it. The dissection then proceeds below the pancreas, with plans for a gastropancreatostomy, extending the dissection to the left of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV).

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and continues dissection behind the pancreas to reveal the portal-splenic junction. A small branch, possibly a transverse vein, is divided. The superior mesenteric vein (SMV) is exposed, with a cyst abutting the SMV at the head of the pancreas. The cyst is large but not adherent to the vein, allowing for easy separation. The origin of the splenic vein is also visible, with the cyst pushing against the portal vein and SMV without adhesion.

The surgeon continues dissection down toward the uncinate process, separating the tissue wall from the vein. The gastrocolic vein is identified and divided early to prevent inadvertent tearing during dissection, using PK energy for cauterization. The duodenum is well exposed, and the dissection progresses toward the left side of the mesentery root, revealing the fourth portion of the duodenum.

The surgeon continues to dissect and mobilize the duodenum and head of the pancreas, pushing the SMV and its branches away from the uncinate process. The cyst first jejunal branch vein is preserved during the dissection. Despite the duodenum being quite free, additional mobilization is attempted by moving to the left side to further mobilize and divide the jejunum.

The surgeon approaches the left side, where the isocolon has been divided, allowing for easy identification of the duodenal-jejunal junction. This portion is mobilized from its retroperitoneal attachment. The transfigurements are nearly free, marking the last attachment before identifying the area for jejunal division.

The surgeon uses a PK device to cauterize individual vessels on the right side of the mesentery, dividing branches to the uncinate process while preserving the first jejunal branch. The focus is on mobilizing the pancreas and duodenum by selectively cutting and cauterizing vessels.

The surgeon carefully separates the mesopancreas from the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), using a sucker to gently push against the vein without applying excessive pressure. Individual vessels are managed with cautery, and the mesopancreas is progressively separated from the artery and vein. The neck remains connected, allowing continued work in the current space.

The surgeon successfully separates the pancreas from the artery and vein, revealing the head of the pancreas completely detached from the vessels. The foramen of Winslow is exposed, with the aorta visible. The lymph node of the hepatoduodenal ligament is identified, along with the superior pancreaticoduodenal vein connecting the pancreas to the portal vein, which is cauterized and divided. The common bile duct is visible, with a clip on the remnant of the cystic duct and artery, and the surgeon prepares to divide the common duct above the cystic duct junction.

The surgeon dissects further to obtain adequate length of the common duct, temporarily clipping it to prevent leakage before division. The cystic duct stump is separated from the cystic artery stump, which is noted to be of a good size. The pancreatic neck is isolated upon visualizing the portal vein, and the surgeon uses a GIA stapler to divide the thin pancreas, cutting one of the branches to the gastroduodenal artery.

The surgeon continues dissection to gain more space, deciding between using stitches, clips, or cauterization for small branches. The gastroduodenal artery (GDA) is clipped and divided, and the pancreatic neck is temporarily divided using a GIA stapler for efficiency. The pancreas is now completely detached, leaving only the GDA and the attachment to the distal common duct, which are sharply dissected and divided, freeing the specimen.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the remaining lymph node attachments, successfully removing the specimen and placing it in a bag. The pancreas is further mobilized to allow rotation and tension-free anastomosis through the stomach. The pancreatic stump is extended to approximately four to five centimeters, stopping at the curve of the splenic artery into the pancreas.

The surgeon revises the pancreatic stump, noting the duct size is approximately four to five millimeters. The posterior wall of the stomach is identified to ensure it lies naturally over the pancreas. The surgeon prepares for an anastomosis using a two-layer technique: first attaching the pancreatic capsule to the seromuscular layer of the stomach, followed by the pancreatic duct mucosa to the gastric mucosa. Silk sutures are used for this procedure, and a gastrotomy is performed with care to avoid making the mucosal opening too large.

The surgeon begins the ductomucosa anastomosis by suturing the pancreatic duct to the gastric mucosa in an interrupted fashion, starting with the posterior wall. The duct is of sufficient size, allowing the procedure to proceed without a stent.

The surgeon uses 3-0 Vicryl sutures to add a few more stitches to the anterior anastomosis, achieving a precise approximation of the pancreatic mucosa to the gastric mucosa. Three interrupted stitches are placed to ensure visibility of the duct mucosa, followed by tying them. The second layer of the anterior anastomosis involves suturing the seromuscular layer to the capsule of the pancreas.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the pancreas, allowing it to be rolled backward without tension. The common duct anastomosis is initiated, with the duct size appearing large enough for a straightforward procedure. A stay suture is placed to stabilize the jejunum, and the posterior layer of the anastomosis is performed using 5-0 PDS sutures in a running fashion. The anterior layer follows after the posterior is completed.

The surgeon performs a duodenal jejunostomy anastomosis, using a two-layer end-to-side technique. The posterior layer is created by suturing the staple line of the duodenum to the seromuscular layer of the jejunum with 3-0 PDS sutures. After completing the posterior seromuscular anastomosis, the duodenum and jejunum are opened for a full-thickness posterior anastomosis. The specimen is then removed through an enlarged umbilical incision.
